summary: Martin Fowler shares his annual list of six musical discoveries from 2022, spanning genres from techno-jazz big band to Mediterranean trance, Afrobeat, English folk, accordion jazz, and manipulated free jazz improvisation. The post is a personal diversion from his usual software topics, highlighting lesser-known artists and albums with links to Bandcamp samples. He also gives an honorable mention to Queen's 1986 Wembley concert recording, reflecting on their unmatched live energy. The piece concludes by crediting his music discovery sources and linking to his other annual music posts.
key_points: Fowler highlights genre-crossing artists like Jazzrausch Bigband (techno meets big band) and Vincent Peirani (accordion in jazz) who challenge instrumental and stylistic boundaries
The selections emphasize global musical cross-pollination: Mediterranean trance, London-based Afrobeat, and New York free jazz with electronica editing
Queen's 1986 Wembley concert gets a special mention as a reminder that studio recordings understate the band's live power
Bandcamp and jazz podcasts serve as Fowler's primary discovery channels for non-mainstream music
money_quotes: Jazzrausch Bigband is compelling evidence that, however unlikely it sounds, Duke Ellington had a baby with Kraftwerk.
The Mediterranean is a fascinating mixing pot: Graeco-Roman classicism, catholic Europe, the Maghreb, Ottoman, and Arabian cultures united by trade over the course of centuries.
With folks like these, Afrobeat has a bright future.
Vincent Peirani lays out proof that it has far wider abilities, capable of playing as central a role in Jazz groups as the piano.

This album was recorded by having a free jazz improvisational session, later edited and manipulated in the studio. The result is music from a dream that combines improvisational melodies with an electronica-enhanced rhythmic texture.
Queen in the studio was like Dread Pirate Roberts fighting left-handed.
Few bands can match their energy, none are fronted by the greatest showman of them all.
key_insight: Musical innovation thrives at the intersection of genres and traditions, and the most compelling artists are those who challenge the boundaries of their instruments and styles.
